Title: Build a Minute-by-Minute Run-of-Show Your Whole Team Can Run
Teaser: Turns a rough event outline into a precise run-of-show with timings, cues, owners, and contingencies, so nothing stalls and everyone knows their next move.
PromptHint: Describe the event and its segments in [EVENT]. Add date/length, format, and team roles in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are an event producer who has saved more live shows than you can count by writing run-of-shows precise enough that a stranger could call the event from the booth. The event:
<event>
[EVENT]
</event>
Duration, format (in-person/virtual/hybrid), platform/venue, and who's on the team and their roles (optional): [CONTEXT]
The one moment most likely to go wrong, if you know it (optional): [RISK]
Think first, silently: lay the event on a single timeline, find the transitions where control passes between people or systems, and pre-decide what happens if each one slips.
Then build the run-of-show: (1) A header block with event name, date, total runtime, and key contacts. (2) The main table, one row per segment: Clock time | Duration | Segment | What happens on screen/stage | Who's speaking | Cue / trigger | Owner (tech, host, backstage). (3) Hard transitions marked clearly (intro, handoffs, Q&A start, close). (4) A 'tech & assets needed' column or list (slides, lower-thirds, polls, videos, music). (5) A short pre-show checklist (T-30 / T-10 / T-1). (6) A contingency box: if a speaker drops, if a demo fails, if you're running long — the exact fallback for each.
CONSTRAINTS: Every row must have one named owner — no unassigned actions. Use real clock times, not just durations, so anyone can see where 'now' should be. Don't pad the agenda to look full; if a segment is thin, flag it. Build in buffer rather than scheduling to the second.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A run-of-show document: header block, the segment table described above, a pre-show checklist, and a 'When things go wrong' contingency list.

Title: Write an Event Invitation That Earns the RSVP
Teaser: Crafts an invite that makes the value obvious in seconds, removes every reason to hesitate, and tells the right person exactly why this is worth their calendar.
PromptHint: Describe the event and who it's for in [EVENT]. Add the date, format, and the single outcome attendees get in [DETAILS].
Prompt:
You are an event marketer who knows an invite competes with a hundred other things in a busy inbox, so you lead with the payoff, not the logistics. The event:
<event>
[EVENT]
</event>
Who it's for, the date/time/timezone, format, and the single most valuable thing they'll walk away with (optional): [DETAILS]
What you want them to do and any deadline or limited capacity (optional): [GOAL]
Think first, silently: decide who must say yes and what they'd have to believe to clear time for this, then build the invite around that belief — not around a list of agenda bullets.
Then write the invitation in three lengths: (1) A full email version — a subject line that signals value (offer 3 options), a first line that names the reader's situation, 2-3 sentences on the concrete payoff and who should attend, the essential details (date, time + timezone, where/how to join, duration), and one clear call to action. (2) A short version for a DM, Slack, or text. (3) A one-line calendar-style blurb for a banner or signature.
CONSTRAINTS: Lead with the outcome, not 'we are excited to invite you'. Specifics over adjectives — no 'amazing', 'unmissable', or invented attendee counts. Always state the timezone and the time commitment. Make the CTA a single, obvious action.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Labelled blocks — 'Email (with 3 subject options)', 'Short message', 'One-liner' — each ready to send as-is.

Title: Design a Full Reminder Sequence That Lifts Attendance
Teaser: Builds the timed string of reminders from registration to 'we're live' that turns sign-ups into actual attendees, each message with a distinct reason to show up.
PromptHint: Describe the event and how people registered in [EVENT]. Add the timeline, format, and channels you can use in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a lifecycle marketer who treats the gap between RSVP and attendance as the real battle, and wins it with reminders that each add a fresh reason rather than repeating 'don't forget'. The event:
<event>
[EVENT]
</event>
When it happens (date/time/timezone), format, how people registered, and which channels you can use (email, SMS, calendar, Slack/WhatsApp) (optional): [CONTEXT]
The biggest reason your registrants tend to no-show (optional): [DROP-OFF REASON]
Think first, silently: map the days between now and go-live, and assign each touchpoint one job — confirm, build anticipation, lower friction, create urgency, or trigger the join — so no two reminders feel the same.
Then build the sequence: (1) A schedule table: Timing (e.g. on registration, T-1 week, T-1 day, T-1 hour, at start) | Channel | Goal of this message | Subject/opening line. (2) The full copy for each touchpoint, short and skimmable, each surfacing a different hook (the takeaway, the speaker, a teaser resource, the join link, 'we're live now'). (3) An 'add to calendar' nudge placed where it helps most. (4) A friction-removal line in the final reminders (exact link, no login needed, runs X minutes). (5) An optional same-day SMS/short-message variant.
CONSTRAINTS: Never send the identical message twice — each must carry a new angle. Put the join link and timezone in every late-stage reminder. Keep last-hour messages to a few lines. Don't fabricate bonuses or scarcity that isn't real.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The schedule table, then each reminder as a labelled block (timing + channel + copy), ending with the SMS variant and a one-line 'sending tips' note.

Title: Write the Host Script That Makes You Sound Calm and In Control
Teaser: Produces a word-for-word host script — open, transitions, speaker intros, Q&A wrangling, and close — so you can run a polished event without freezing or rambling.
PromptHint: Describe the event and your role in [EVENT]. Add the agenda, speakers, and your hosting style in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a seasoned event host and writer who knows the audience relaxes the moment the host sounds relaxed, so you script the words that make hosting feel effortless. The event you're hosting:
<event>
[EVENT]
</event>
The agenda/segments, speaker names and one-line bios, your role, and the tone you want (warm, sharp, high-energy, understated) (optional): [CONTEXT]
What you tend to fumble live — openings, intros, dead air, wrapping up (optional): [WEAK SPOT]
Think first, silently: identify the moments that make hosts stumble (the cold open, the handoff, the silence after 'any questions?', the goodbye) and write words that carry you smoothly through each.
Then write the script: (1) The open — a warm welcome, why everyone's here, what they'll get, and the housekeeping (mute, captions, recording, where to drop questions). (2) Speaker introductions — a tight, flattering line or two each, ending with a clean handoff sentence. (3) Transition lines for moving between segments and reclaiming the room. (4) Q&A management — how to open it, how to bridge silence, how to redirect a rambling question, how to park an off-topic one gracefully. (5) The close — recap the one thing to remember, the call to action / next step, the thank-yous, and the sign-off. (6) Bracketed [pause] / [advance slide] / [unmute speaker] stage directions.
CONSTRAINTS: Write spoken English — short sentences, contractions, easy to say out loud. Give real words to say, not '[welcome the audience]'. Include filler-busting bridge lines for awkward gaps. Keep it natural to your stated tone, not corporate.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A scripted document in running order with clear section labels and bracketed stage directions, plus a one-page 'cheat-sheet' of the key lines (open, each handoff, Q&A bridges, close) for quick glancing during the live event.

Title: Build a Sponsor Pitch Deck Outline That Closes the Partnership
Teaser: Turns your event into a sponsor-ready deck — audience proof, package tiers, and ROI framing — built to answer the only question a sponsor asks: what do we get?
PromptHint: Describe your event and audience in [EVENT]. Add sponsorship goals, budget range, and what you can offer in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a partnerships lead who has read enough sponsor decks to know they fail when they sell the event instead of the sponsor's return, so you build every slide around 'what's in it for them'. The event:
<event>
[EVENT]
</event>
Your audience (size, who they are, why a sponsor wants them), what you can offer (logo, stage time, booth, email, social, data), and any package/price ideas (optional): [CONTEXT]
The type of sponsor you're targeting and what they care about (optional): [SPONSOR TYPE]
Think first, silently: from the sponsor's seat, work out what they're really buying — reach, credibility, leads, or association — and make the deck prove that, not just describe the event.
Then outline the deck, slide by slide: (1) Cover — event name, date, and a one-line promise to sponsors. (2) The opportunity — why this audience is hard to reach elsewhere. (3) Audience proof — who attends, in numbers and segments (use placeholders where you lack data, clearly marked). (4) What sponsorship gets them — tied to their goals (visibility / leads / authority). (5) Packages — tiered table (e.g. Title / Gold / Community) with what's included and indicative price, plus what's exclusive to each. (6) Activation ideas — concrete ways they show up that attendees actually like. (7) Proof / credibility — past results, testimonials, or named partners (placeholders if new). (8) The ask & next step — what to do, by when, and who to contact.
CONSTRAINTS: Frame every benefit as sponsor ROI, not event features. Do not invent audience numbers, past results, or logos — insert clearly marked [PLACEHOLDER] for anything you'd need to confirm. Make tiers genuinely different, not just 'more of the same'. Keep it skimmable — a sponsor decides in minutes.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A slide-by-slide outline (slide title + the bullets/visual for each), a packages comparison table, and a short 'data to fill in before sending' list of every placeholder used.

Title: Write Post-Event Follow-Up That Converts Attendees Into Next Steps
Teaser: Drafts the segmented after-event emails — to attendees, no-shows, and hot leads — that deliver the promised value and move each group toward the action you want.
PromptHint: Describe the event and what you offered in [EVENT]. Add the next step you want and the recording/resources in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a follow-up strategist who knows most of an event's value is won or lost in the 48 hours after it ends, so you write the after-emails that turn attention into action while it's still warm. The event that just happened:
<event>
[EVENT]
</event>
What you promised attendees, the recording/slides/resources you can share, and the next step you want them to take (book a call, buy, sign up, reply) (optional): [CONTEXT]
The key moment or takeaway from the event worth referencing (optional): [HIGHLIGHT]
Think first, silently: the people on your list aren't one audience — attendees, no-shows, and engaged leads need different messages, so decide what each group already knows and what would move them.
Then write the follow-ups: (1) Attendees — thank them, deliver the promised resources up front, recap the single most valuable point, and make one clear next-step offer. (2) No-shows — acknowledge they missed it without guilt, lead with the recording/resource, and give them the same next step. (3) Engaged / hot leads (asked a question, stayed to the end, clicked) — a warmer, more personal note that references their interest and makes a more direct ask. (4) A subject line set (2-3 options) for each. (5) A short timing note: when to send each and whether a single nudge follow-up is worth it.
CONSTRAINTS: Deliver the value before the ask — link the resource near the top. Keep each email short enough to read on a phone. One primary call to action per email. Don't over-claim ('you'll never…') or fabricate scarcity. Match the tone of the event.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Three labelled emails (Attendees / No-shows / Hot leads), each with subject options and body, plus a 'send timing' note at the end.

Title: Turn Your Event Into a Week of Recap Content
Teaser: Mines a single event for a full content slate — recap post, key-takeaways thread, quote cards, clips, and a summary article — so one event fuels weeks of reach.
PromptHint: Paste the event topic, transcript, or key points in [EVENT CONTENT]. Add your platforms and audience in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a content strategist who treats every event as a quarry, not a moment — one good session should yield a week of posts that reach the people who never attended. The event material:
<event_content>
[EVENT CONTENT]
</event_content>
Where you'll publish (LinkedIn, X/Twitter, Instagram, blog, newsletter, YouTube), your audience, and your brand voice (optional): [CONTEXT]
The single most quotable or surprising moment, if you know it (optional): [STANDOUT MOMENT]
Think first, silently: pull out the 5-7 ideas, quotes, and moments that can each stand alone, then match each to the platform and format where it'll travel furthest.
Then produce the content slate: (1) A recap post (LinkedIn-style) — what happened, the top insight, and a hook to watch or attend next time. (2) A key-takeaways thread (X/Twitter-style) — 5-8 numbered points, each a self-contained lesson. (3) Three quote-card lines — short, punchy, designed to sit on an image. (4) Two short-clip ideas — the timestamp/moment to cut and the caption that frames it. (5) A short summary article outline for a blog or newsletter — headline, subheads, and the one CTA. (6) A repurposing calendar — what to post on which day across the week.
CONSTRAINTS: Every piece must stand alone — no 'as we discussed in the event'. Quote and paraphrase faithfully; don't invent statements the speakers didn't make. Match each format's native style and length. One clear takeaway or CTA per piece — no kitchen-sink posts.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Labelled sections for each asset (Recap post / Takeaways thread / Quote cards / Clip ideas / Article outline), then a 'this week's posting calendar' table (Day | Platform | Asset).

Title: Design a Post-Event Survey That Gives You Answers You Can Act On
Teaser: Builds a short, sharp feedback survey that measures what actually matters and produces decisions, not a pile of vague ratings nobody knows what to do with.
PromptHint: Describe the event and what you want to learn in [EVENT]. Add the audience and any decision riding on the results in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a research-minded event lead who knows a bad survey wastes the attendee's goodwill and yours, so you ask only the questions whose answers will change what you do next. The event:
<event>
[EVENT]
</event>
Who attended, what you most want to learn (content quality, format, intent to return, NPS, lead interest), and any decision the results will inform (optional): [CONTEXT]
The biggest open question you have about how it went (optional): [KEY QUESTION]
Think first, silently: for each thing you're tempted to ask, decide what you'd actually do differently based on the answer — and cut any question that fails that test.
Then build the survey: (1) A one-line intro that says how long it takes and why it helps (keep it under ~2 minutes). (2) An overall satisfaction or NPS question as the anchor. (3) 3-5 targeted questions tied to your decisions — mixing rating scales with one or two specifics (e.g. 'which segment was most valuable?', 'what would have made this a 10?'). (4) One open-ended question that invites the comment you can't get from a scale. (5) An optional intent/next-step question (would attend again / want to be contacted), with a clear opt-in. (6) A thank-you close with what happens next.
CONSTRAINTS: Keep it short — every extra question lowers completion. No double-barrelled or leading questions ('how amazing was…'). Make scales consistent and labelled. Always include at least one open text field. Mark which questions are required vs optional, and make the contact/opt-in genuinely optional.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A ready-to-load survey: intro line, the numbered questions with their answer types (scale 1-5 / multiple choice with options / open text), required/optional tags, and the thank-you message — plus a short 'what each question tells you' note mapping questions to the decisions they inform.

Title: Plan a Webinar From Topic to Go-Live Without the Chaos
Teaser: Converts a webinar idea into a complete plan — promise, agenda, promo timeline, tech checklist, and assets — so you arrive at go-live calm and fully prepared.
PromptHint: Describe the webinar topic and goal in [WEBINAR]. Add the date, audience, and platform in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a webinar producer who has learned that a great webinar is mostly decided before anyone goes live, so you plan backward from go-live and leave nothing to the day-of scramble. The webinar:
<webinar>
[WEBINAR]
</webinar>
The date/time/timezone, target audience, platform, presenter(s), and your goal (leads, education, sales, community) (optional): [CONTEXT]
What tends to fall apart when you run these (optional): [PAIN POINT]
Think first, silently: define the single promise of the title — what the attendee can do or understand afterward — and make every part of the plan serve that promise, then work backward from go-live to today.
Then build the plan: (1) The core promise and a title/subtitle that sells it. (2) The agenda — a tight segment breakdown with rough minutes, ending with a clear CTA segment. (3) A promotion timeline working back from the date (when to announce, remind, and push) across your channels. (4) The assets you'll need (registration page copy points, slides outline, poll/CTA ideas, email touchpoints). (5) A tech & rehearsal checklist (platform settings, audio/video test, screen-share, backup host, recording on). (6) A day-of timeline (T-60 to live). (7) The follow-up plan in one line (recording + next step).
CONSTRAINTS: Tie every segment and asset back to the core promise; cut anything that doesn't serve it. Don't over-schedule the live time — leave room for Q&A and overrun. Flag the single highest-risk dependency (usually tech or a speaker) and its backup. Use real time markers, not vague 'before the event'.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A planning document with the labelled sections above — including the agenda as a timed table and a working-back promo timeline as a dated checklist — and a one-line 'biggest risk + backup' callout.

Title: Engineer the Live Engagement Plan So Your Webinar Isn't a Monologue
Teaser: Designs the polls, prompts, chat moments, and Q&A flow that keep a live audience awake, participating, and present to the end instead of quietly dropping off.
PromptHint: Describe the session and audience in [SESSION]. Add the length, platform, and where attention usually drops in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a live-event facilitator who knows attention is a leaking bucket, so you place interaction at exactly the points where people would otherwise drift off. The session:
<session>
[SESSION]
</session>
Duration, platform (and its interaction tools — chat, polls, reactions, breakout, Q&A), audience size and type, and the content's natural beats (optional): [CONTEXT]
Where you usually feel the energy sag (optional): [SAG POINT]
Think first, silently: map the session's energy curve, predict where attention dips (after the intro, mid-content, before Q&A), and design a participation moment for each dip that fits the platform's tools.
Then build the engagement plan: (1) An opening interaction in the first 2-3 minutes that gets everyone clicking/typing once (a poll, a chat question, a hand-raise). (2) A timeline of engagement beats mapped to the agenda: timestamp | what you ask | tool used | what you do with the responses. (3) 3-5 ready-to-use poll/chat prompts written out, each with a purpose. (4) A Q&A plan — how to seed it early, how to collect questions during (not just at the end), and a few 'priming' questions to ask if the queue is empty. (5) Energy-revival tactics for the mid-session lull (a story, a poll reveal, a callout). (6) A line for using responses live ('I see 60% said X — that's exactly why…').
CONSTRAINTS: Tie each interaction to content — no engagement for its own sake. Don't over-schedule prompts so they interrupt flow; space them to the energy dips. Write the actual prompts, not 'ask a poll'. Have a fallback for low response ('if the chat is quiet, I'll…'). Keep it doable for one host.
OUTPUT FORMAT: An engagement plan: the timeline table (timestamp | prompt | tool | use of responses), a list of the written-out poll/chat prompts, the Q&A seeding plan, and a short 'if the room goes quiet' fallback list.

Title: Write the On-the-Day Comms Pack: Logistics, Joins, and 'We're Live'
Teaser: Generates every short message you send on event day — joining instructions, the go-live alert, a delay or tech-issue note, and the on-screen housekeeping slide.
PromptHint: Describe the event and how people join in [EVENT]. Add the platform, start time/timezone, and any quirks in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are an event operations lead who knows the difference between a smooth event and a panicked one is having the day-of messages already written, so you prepare every comms moment before go-live. The event:
<event>
[EVENT]
</event>
The platform and exact join method, start time + timezone, expected audience, and any known quirks (login needed, waiting room, captions, regional access) (optional): [CONTEXT]
The day-of problem you most dread (optional): [WORRY]
Think first, silently: walk the attendee's day-of journey from 'how do I join?' to 'it's started' to 'something's wrong', and pre-write the message for each moment so you're never typing under pressure.
Then write the comms pack: (1) Joining instructions — the exact steps and link, what to expect, when to arrive, and a 'trouble joining? do this' line. (2) The 'we're live now' alert — a short, energetic nudge for email/SMS/Slack with the one-tap link. (3) A 'starting shortly / slight delay' holding message that keeps people from leaving. (4) A tech-issue message — calm, honest, with what to do (refresh, rejoin, where the recording will be). (5) An on-screen / waiting-room housekeeping blurb (mute, captions, where to ask questions, recording notice). (6) A 'thanks for joining, here's what's next' immediate-close message. (7) For each: the channel it's best suited to.
CONSTRAINTS: Keep every message short and scannable — these are read in seconds, often on a phone. Put the join link and timezone in every relevant message. Stay calm and reassuring in the problem messages — never blame the platform or panic. Don't promise fixes or timings you can't guarantee.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A labelled comms pack — Join instructions / We're live / Holding-delay / Tech issue / Housekeeping blurb / Thanks-and-next — each with its suggested channel, ready to copy and send.

Title: Build the Post-Event Wrap Report That Proves the Event Was Worth It
Teaser: Turns scattered event numbers and notes into a clear wrap report — results against goals, what worked, what to fix, and the recommendation for next time.
PromptHint: Paste your event results and notes in [EVENT DATA]. Add the original goals and who the report is for in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are an events analyst who knows a wrap report's job isn't to list numbers but to answer 'did this work, and should we do it again?', so you write for the person holding the budget. The event and its data:
<event_data>
[EVENT DATA]
</event_data>
The original goals/targets, who the report is for (you, your boss, a sponsor, the wider team), and any context on cost or effort (optional): [CONTEXT]
The decision this report should inform (optional): [DECISION]
Think first, silently: anchor everything to the goals you set out with — registrations vs attendance, engagement, leads, satisfaction, cost — and separate what the numbers say from what you think they mean.
Then write the wrap report: (1) Executive summary — 3-4 sentences a busy leader absorbs instantly: did it hit goal, the standout result, and your headline recommendation. (2) Results vs goals — a table of each target, the actual, and a hit/miss/partial verdict. (3) The funnel — invited to registered to attended to engaged to converted (whatever you have), with the drop-off points flagged. (4) What worked — 2-4 things to keep, with the evidence. (5) What to fix — 2-4 specific, actionable changes for next time. (6) Attendee sentiment — the gist of feedback, the recurring praise and gripes. (7) Recommendation — repeat / adjust / drop, and the single biggest lever for next time.
CONSTRAINTS: Tie every claim to a number or a quote where you have one; where you don't, mark it as an impression, not a fact. Don't spin a weak result — name misses plainly and turn them into fixes. Keep the summary genuinely short. If you're missing data needed for a verdict, say what's missing rather than guessing.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A wrap report with the labelled sections above — including a 'Results vs goals' table (Goal | Target | Actual | Verdict) and a 'What to fix next time' action list — ending with a one-line recommendation and a 'data I'd want next time' note.
